Author: Soriano JR. MD, Sofronio Sagucio
This is a case of a 66-year-old male with a significant past medical history of type 2
diabetes mellitus, prior cerebrovascular accident with residual left-sided deficits,
peripheral arterial disease, and chronic urinary obstruction secondary to prostate
hyperplasia, who presented to the hospital on 2/19/2026 via EMS for inability to care for
himself and concern for multiple infected wounds.
According to available documentation and EMS report, the patient was found sitting in his
wheelchair covered in stool and urine, raising concerns regarding severe functional
decline and inability to performactivities of daily living independently. The patient
reportedly lives alone at home and is wheel bound at baseline due to residual
deficits froma prior CVA approximately five years ago, including persistent left-sided
weakness and left upper extremity contracture.
On initial evaluation in the energency department, the patient was noted to have multiple
wounds, ncluding a large infected wound of the left thigh and an ulcerated wound along
the lateral aspect of the right foot, with associated soft-tissue swelling and concern for
Initial I aboratory evaluation demonstrated eukocytosis, anemia, elevated creatinine,
elevated CK, elevated CRP, and mildly elevated lactic acid. Respiratory viral testing was
Imaging studies revealed a large rimenhancing fluid collection measuring approximately
13.9 X 10.7 cmalong the lateral aspect of the left thigh concerning for abscess formation
with internal air suggesting possible comunication with the skin or necrotizing
infection. Imaging also demonstrated moderate to severe bilateral hydronephrosis and
adder distention with marked omegaly consistent with chronic urinary outlet
Lower extremity vascular studies showed advanced peripheral arterial sease
absent flowin the right posterior tibial artery and monophasi c distal waveforms
The patient was initially treated with broad-spectrum antibiotics and admitted for further
management. Multiple services were consulted ncl general surgery, orthopedics,
Encompass Health Rehabilitation Hospital of Las Vegas
infectious disease, psychiatry, vascular surgery, wound care, and interventional
During hospitalization the patient underwent surgical debridement of the left thigh
abscess on 2/20/2026 which reveal extension into the joint consistent with septic
arthritis and gangrenous necrotic f emur invol vement. A wound VAC was aced
post-operatively. Intraoperative wound cultures grew Proteus vul garis and Streptococcus
dysgalactiae. Infectious sease recommended long-term antibiotic therapy consisting of
ceftriaxone 2 IV daily and metronidazole 500 mg POthree times daily for a planned six
The patient also under went vascular intervention for severe peripheral arterial disease
including right superficial femoral artery and popliteal artery drug-coated balloon
angioplasty, right per oneal artery balloon angioplasty, and right SFA stent lacement.
Attempts to recanalize the left SFA chronic total occlusion were unsuccessful. The patient
subsequently under went left lower extremity angiogram with posterior tibial artery
During the admission the patient devel oped acute kidney injury secondary to post renal
obstruction withimaging demonstrating bilateral hydronephrosis and adder distention due
to marked prostatomegaly. Urology was consulted and Foley catheter placement was
performed with improvement in renal function and resolution of the AKI.
The patient's hospital course was further complicated by anemia with hemogl obin dropping
to 6.9 on 3/8 requiring transfusion of one unit of packed red ood cells with Improvement
Given the patient's significant functional decline, need for wound care, IV antibiotics,
and rehabilitation, discharge planning involved acement in a rehabilitation facility for
coordinated tidisciplinary care Including physical therapy, occupational therapy,
wound management, and continued infectious disease treatment.
The patient's prior functional levels are supervision to modified ndependent level with bed
mobility and also transfers and ambulation ADLs.
The patient's current functional levels are maximal assistance with bed mobility and transfers and
The patient is currently requiring maximal assistance with upper and lower body dressing and
bathing and toileting and grooming and hygiene.

Twel systems were reviewed. The patient complains of pain, generalized weakness, easy
fatigability, poor endurance and shortness of breath and unsteady gait. AII other review of systems
were unremarkable except as described in the history of present illness.
HEENT: Slightly pale unctivae. Anicteric sclerae. Extraocular muscles intact. Micous membranes
LUNGS: Harsh breath sounds, but no wheeze or rales.
HEART: Nor mal rate and regular rhythm
ABDOMEN: Soft and nont ender with normactive bowel sounds.
EXTREM TI ES: There is edema noted to the Lower extremities.
NEUROMUSCULAR: The patient is awake and alert. The patient is able to follow one to two step
commands. There are no obvious cranial nerve abnormalities.

The patient has impairment in bed mobility, transfers, unsteady gait and ance, easy fatigability
and poor endurance and pain and overall loss of functional ndependence. Because of above medical
problems and functional issues, it is my opinion the patient shoul be in acute rehab unit rather
than a ower level of setting of care. Therefore, she will need multidisciplinary approach, three
hours a day of therapy for five days a week, close medical supervision for 24 hours, and 24- hour
There are potential medical risks that the patient can encounter while in an acuteinpatient
High risk of developing DVT to the Lower extremities.
Hypogi ycemic epi sodes and Hyperg ycemic sodes
The patient will benefit from intensive rehabilitation program (3 hours per day, 5 days a week).
Considering all the information above, it is my best udgment that this patient requires intensive
rehabilitation therapy as described above in an inpatient hospital environment due to complexity of
nursing, medical management and rehabilitation needs. Further more, this patient can reasonably be
expected to participate in and benefit from an inpatient stay with an interdi plinary team
approach to the delivery of rehabilitation care under the direction and supervision of a
1. Impairment with ambulation and mobility: Continue physical therapy.
2. Impairment of activities of daily living: Continue occupational therapy.
5. Check laboratory data on a regular basis.
6. WII treat the patient as the clinical course unfolds.
8. Monitor serum electrolytes and kidney function tests as needed
REHABILITATION GOALS: Rehabilitation goals are to maximize patient's ndependence with ADLs, self
care, mobility, comunication skills and patient's medical status. Specific goals are to be
established following completion of the assessment period. Goals incl ude:
